stories.headline: Update: Aspen Acres Fire Grows to 97,000 Acres, 850 Structures Destroyed as Containment Reaches 28%
stories.summary:
The Aspen Acres Fire burning in Pueblo and Custer counties has grown to approximately 97,083 acres with 28% containment as of July 10, according to the Colorado Sun's wildfire tracker. More than 850 structures have been destroyed in southern Colorado, a significant increase from earlier reports, and mandatory evacuations remain in effect for communities including Beulah, Colorado City and Wetmore. A complex incident management team from Alaska has assumed command of the fire, and more than 1,900 personnel were working the blaze as of July 10.

Beulah volunteer firefighter Tom Laca, a captain with the Beulah Fire Protection and Ambulance District, described the moment the wind shifted on June 29 and flames began racing downhill across 12 Mile Road, engulfing the community. About one-third of Beulah's 27 volunteer firefighters lost their own homes while continuing to fight the fire, according to department spokesperson Jill Laca. The historic 1930s-built Horseshoe Lodge, a central community gathering place, was hollowed by the fire, its roof destroyed.

The Gold Mountain Fire near Ouray, now 33,185 acres at 7% containment, also remains active, with Gov. Jared Polis having declared a disaster emergency for that fire. The Ferris Fire complex in San Juan National Forest has grown to 62,916 acres at 19% containment. Water managers in affected communities are beginning the long process of restoring clean water supplies following the blazes.

stories.headline: Denver Police Officer Fired After 16th Street Mall Assault; Second Officer Cited for Reckless Driving
stories.summary:
The Denver Police Department announced Thursday that Officer Gabriel Lucero was fired Wednesday following a May 22 incident in which he and others allegedly assaulted an individual on the 16th Street Mall at approximately 1 a.m. After the assault, security staff and an off-duty officer escorted Lucero's group from the business. Lucero then attempted to use his police badge to regain entry and provided a false name when questioned. He was cited for third-degree assault, official misconduct and false reporting.

Lucero, who joined the department in 2025, was on employment probation at the time of the incident. In a separate case, Officer Javon Leach was cited for reckless driving and eluding after allegedly driving the wrong way out of a parking lot on Larimer Street near Coors Field early on June 21, then driving at high speed on Park Avenue West while ignoring officers' lights and sirens during an attempted traffic stop. Leach has been placed on off-line assignment pending an administrative review.

The department said the administrative review of Leach's case will proceed after the judicial process concludes. The two incidents, coming within weeks of each other and both occurring in downtown Denver, represent separate internal accountability actions by the department.

stories.headline: $110 Million Recycling Facility Opens in Aurora as Colorado Prepares Statewide Recycling Expansion
stories.summary:
WM, formerly Waste Management and Colorado's largest trash company, held a grand opening Thursday for its Denver East Recycling Facility and Hauling Site in Aurora, a $110 million investment capable of processing 175,000 tons of material per year. Gov. Jared Polis attended the ceremony alongside the Colorado Rockies mascot Dinger. The facility features two miles of conveyor belts and automated sorting machines that use light sensors, magnets and air jets to separate plastics, aluminum, cardboard and other recyclables.

The new facility is timed to coincide with Colorado's Producer Responsibility Program, approved last year by the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment, which will require companies that generate packaging waste — such as Amazon — to fund free curbside recycling for more than 700,000 homes and businesses statewide by 2035. Colorado currently has one of the worst recycling rates in the nation. Fees assessed to packaging producers will vary based on the volume and type of waste they create, incentivizing easier-to-recycle packaging designs.

WM officials said the facility upgrade was driven by both population growth and the shift in the types of waste being handled — less newsprint and far more heavy cardboard from e-commerce deliveries. The company is also planning an on-site plant to convert methane from a nearby landfill into enough compressed natural gas to power the equivalent of 15,000 homes.

stories.headline: Colorado Springs Man Sues Therapist After Falling from Fourth-Floor Hotel Window During Psilocybin Session
stories.summary:
A Colorado Springs man has filed a lawsuit in El Paso County District Court alleging that he fell from a fourth-floor hotel window last year after his psilocybin therapist administered a dose of the drug and then left him alone in the room. Jacob Ramirez survived the May 16, 2025, fall from the Spark by Hilton Hotel on Commerce Center Drive but spent nearly two months hospitalized with serious head and chest injuries. His medical bills have exceeded $2 million, according to his attorney, Jared Mazzei.

The lawsuit names counselor Rachel McGuire, her husband Sheldon McGuire and their two therapy companies, A Sparrow's Way and Restoration Counseling. According to the complaint, Rachel McGuire provided Ramirez psilocybin tea at approximately 3:45 p.m. and left the hotel by 5:14 p.m. Security footage and police records indicate Ramirez fell from the window at approximately 6:34 p.m. A bystander administered CPR until first responders arrived.

Colorado Springs police investigated the incident but did not recommend criminal charges. Ramirez's attorney said his client has no memory of the fall and there was no indication of a suicide attempt, describing it instead as a foreseeable consequence of leaving someone unmonitored while under the influence of a powerful psychedelic. The case raises questions about oversight standards for psilocybin-assisted therapy, which Colorado voters approved in 2022.

stories.headline: Denver Housing Market Shows Buyers in Control as Inventory Climbs and Fixer-Uppers Languish
stories.summary:
Denver's housing market posted a median sales price of $616,000 in June, essentially flat from May and up less than 1% from a year ago, according to the Denver Metro Association of Realtors June Market Trends Report. Active listings climbed roughly 4% to 12,744, near decade highs, giving buyers more options and less urgency to act quickly. Closed sales fell approximately 7% month over month to 3,924 homes.

Homes are taking longer to sell, with median days on market rising nearly 29% from a year ago to 18 days for the region. Detached homes averaged 14 days on market, up 27% from May, while attached homes averaged 34 days, a 17% jump. DMAR Market Trends Committee chair Amanda Snitker said buyers are now scrutinizing property condition more than ever, including roof age, water heaters and windows, before considering kitchens or other cosmetic features.

The luxury segment — homes priced at $1 million or more — has seen more closed sales year to date than any of the previous three years but is also taking longer to sell, with median days on market up nearly 17% from last year. Real estate professionals say sellers who have maintained their homes as move-in ready are seeing faster results, while properties needing work are sitting on the market with little buyer interest.

stories.headline: Nuggets Offseason Quiet Amid LeBron Pursuit, Peyton Watson Decision and Looming Luxury Tax
stories.summary:
More than a week into NBA free agency, the Denver Nuggets have made relatively few moves, leaving fans and league observers puzzled. The team has waived Jonas Valanciunas, signed veterans Marvin Bagley III and Tyus Jones to one-year deals and watched Tim Hardaway Jr. depart for Miami, but has stopped short of the significant trade activity many anticipated given the franchise's mounting payroll concerns. Team president Josh Kroenke had declared in May that "everything is on the table" outside of trading Nikola Jokic.

Denver remains in discussions around LeBron James, who left the Los Angeles Lakers, though the Nuggets face significant financial constraints in making a competitive offer. The team also must decide on restricted free agent Peyton Watson, whom the Nuggets have told league sources they intend to match any qualifying offer when the NBA moratorium lifts Monday. Denver's guaranteed salary commitments for eight players currently on contract stand at approximately $203.4 million, with a potential roster bill exceeding $456 million for 2026-27 if Watson and other situations are resolved at market rates.

At Denver's Summer League opener Friday in Las Vegas, a 97-86 loss to Houston, Nuggets executive Nico Harrison said the organization feels confident about its direction and is not feeling rushed into making moves. Draft picks Bryce Hopkins led the team with 24 points and Trevon Brazile signed his contract with the team on Friday. Hopkins had yet to finalize his contract heading into Denver's second Summer League game Saturday.

stories.headline: Rockies Prospect Roldy Brito Headed to MLB All-Star Futures Game as One of Three Low-A Selections
stories.summary:
Colorado Rockies prospect Roldy Brito, a 19-year-old outfielder and second baseman, will represent the organization Sunday at the 2026 MLB All-Star Futures Game in Philadelphia, one of just three Low-A players selected for the showcase. Brito, the Rockies' third-ranked prospect and No. 71 on MLB's overall top-100 list, has slashed .328/.403/.464 across more than 110 games and 836 career at-bats since signing as a non-drafted international free agent from the Dominican Republic in January 2024.

Brito said he set participation in the Futures Game as a personal goal before the season began, telling his father about the ambition ahead of the year. His father, Ronny Brito, played in the Philadelphia Phillies' minor league system and has served as his son's primary coach and mentor. Brito was named the 2025 Doug Million Minor League Player of the Year and MVP of the Arizona Complex League last season.

Brito joins Triple-A pitcher Charlie Condon as the two Rockies representatives in this year's Futures Game. His rapid ascent through the system has drawn attention across the industry, and he is widely viewed as one of the more promising positional prospects in the Rockies' pipeline during a rebuilding period for the organization.

stories.headline: Rockies Face Uncertainty Heading Into MLB Trade Deadline as Front Office Signals Flexible Approach
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies are approaching the MLB trade deadline without a clear, fixed strategy, with the front office signaling flexibility on which players could be moved or retained as the deadline nears. The team has been discussed in trade rumors amid its rebuilding phase, and decision-makers have not publicly committed to either aggressive dealing or holding their roster together. Colorado has outscored opponents 48-43 across six recent high-scoring games against San Francisco, a stretch in which starter Ryan Feltner has allowed no more than two earned runs in four of his last five outings.

The Rockies' willingness to engage in trade conversations reflects a broader organizational posture of balancing near-term results with long-term development. Upcoming prospects like Roldy Brito and Charlie Condon, both appearing in this weekend's Futures Game, underscore the depth the organization is cultivating even as the major league club continues to struggle in the National League West.

The deadline presents both opportunity and risk for Colorado, which must weigh the value of veterans on expiring contracts against the development needs of a young roster. Front office officials have not ruled out moves in either direction — acquiring players to address specific needs or dealing veterans to accelerate the rebuild.

stories.headline: Cyclospora Parasite Detected in Colorado, But State Health Officials Say Cases Are Within Normal Range
stories.summary:
Colorado health officials say the state is not experiencing an unusual number of cyclosporiasis cases despite national headlines linking Colorado to a broader outbreak. The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment has recorded approximately 90 cases of the intestinal parasite so far this year, consistent with the five-year average, according to Deputy Chief Medical Officer Dr. Rachel Herlihy. The actual outbreak of cyclosporiasis is centered in Michigan, where more than 1,562 cases have been reported.

The cyclospora parasite is typically transmitted through contaminated fresh produce, particularly leafy greens that are not cooked before consumption. Unlike many pathogens, it does not spread directly from person to person — shed parasites require at least one to two weeks outside the human body before becoming infectious again, severely limiting its ability to circulate through a community. Washing produce helps reduce but does not eliminate the risk.

Health officials attributed much of the public concern to media coverage that conflated the Michigan outbreak with unrelated cases appearing on a CDC tracking map for other states. Colorado's most common intestinal parasitic disease remains giardia, which sickens roughly twice as many residents annually as cyclospora. Officials are urging residents to wash fresh produce thoroughly but say no special precautions beyond standard food safety practices are needed.

stories.headline: Goat Named Goldie Shadows Colorado Springs Firefighters During Wildfire Response, Providing Levity
stories.summary:
A 4-year-old Nigerian dwarf goat named Goldie — short for Golden Oreo — became an unexpected companion to firefighters battling the Rock Creek Fire in Colorado Springs this week, following crews for hours as they actively worked the blaze and cleared brush. Colorado Springs Fire Department Lt. Trevor Leland said Goldie led crews down a hill, followed them to their trucks and even tried to sneak a bite of a U.S. Forest Service crew member's lunch. The fire reached 50% containment by late Friday.

Owner Lindsey Glader said Goldie is well known for her sociability and described the goat as providing "comedic relief" and "necessary levity" to crews who have been working long shifts on fires across Colorado. Firefighters from Colorado Springs have also responded to the Aspen Acres Fire in Pueblo and Custer counties, where conditions have been far more destructive. Glader praised the firefighters' work in containing the Rock Creek Fire.

The story drew attention amid an unusually active wildfire season in Colorado, which has seen multiple large fires burning simultaneously in the southern and western parts of the state. Authorities attributed the fire conditions to a record lack of snow, high temperatures and erratic winds.

stories.headline: Colorado Black Arts Festival and French Fest Among Highlights for Denver Weekend of July 11-12
stories.summary:
The Colorado Black Arts Festival runs through Sunday at City Park, 1700 City Park Esplanade, offering free admission to live gospel, jazz, blues and hip-hop performances across three stages, alongside vendors selling paintings, prints, jewelry, clothing and beauty products celebrating African American culture. The festival is one of several major events taking place in the Denver metro area this weekend.

Also running this weekend is the Crested Butte Wildflower Festival, a 10-day event through July 19 featuring guided hikes, workshops in wildflower arranging, photography, watercolor, botany, cooking and jeep tours across the region's backcountry. The Children's Museum of Denver is hosting its Mess Fest through Sunday, with daily sensory-play themes including goo, paint and shaving cream at its Marsico Campus location; admission is $20 for children 2 and older.

The Rocky Mountain Regional Rodeo runs through Saturday at the Jefferson County Fairgrounds in Golden, and the Rooftop Rodeo in Estes Park wraps up its run on Friday. The Circus Golden Star, a traveling Finnish circus combining European technique with American showmanship, continues through Saturday at the Greeley Mall.

stories.headline: Santa Fe Drive in Denver to Close This Weekend for Bridge Installation Between Evans and Florida Avenues
stories.summary:
Santa Fe Drive in Denver will close this weekend for installation of a new bridge between Evans and Florida avenues, the city announced. The closure is expected to affect drivers traveling through the south Denver corridor and will require the use of alternate routes during the construction period. The project is part of ongoing infrastructure improvements in the area.

The bridge installation represents a significant disruption to one of Denver's busier north-south arterials, which serves residential neighborhoods, the arts district and retail corridors in the vicinity. City officials have not specified the expected duration of the full closure beyond the weekend work window. Drivers are advised to plan alternate routes and allow additional travel time.

The project adds to a series of infrastructure-related traffic impacts in the Denver metro area during the summer construction season. Residents near the affected stretch of Santa Fe Drive have been notified of the closure through city communications.

weather.notable: Nearly 7 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I tried to explain the internet to my dad. He said, 'So it's like a library?' I said, 'Yeah, if everyone in the library was screaming.'

topStory.headline: Trump Threatens to 'Decimate' Iran After Assassination Calls at Khamenei Funeral; Talks Continue
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump issued a sweeping military threat against Iran late Friday after mourners at the funeral of slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei displayed banners openly calling for Trump's death alongside that of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. In a post on his Truth Social platform, Trump declared that "1,000 missiles are locked and loaded and aimed at the Islamic Republic of Iran" and warned that U.S. forces stood ready "to completely decimate and destroy all areas of Iran" should any attempt on his life be carried out. The threat came amid an Israeli intelligence warning, reported by CNN and The Wall Street Journal, of a fresh and highly specific Iranian plot to assassinate the president, which reportedly prompted the U.S. Secret Service to reroute Trump's aircraft while he was departing a NATO summit in Türkiye earlier in the week.

The funeral for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, 86, who was killed in a U.S. airstrike on Feb. 28 that opened the current conflict, concluded this week after a dayslong ceremony that moved his body through cities in both Iran and Iraq. Iran's new Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei — son of the slain leader — issued a statement Saturday pledging to "exact revenge" for his father's death and the deaths of what he called martyrs of two wars, a reference widely interpreted to mean the recent conflicts with the United States and Israel. He warned that those responsible, whose "names are fully documented from the top to the bottom of the ladder," would not die peacefully.

The current crisis was triggered Tuesday when Iran struck commercial tankers near the Strait of Hormuz, breaking a ceasefire and prompting U.S. airstrikes on Iran for two consecutive nights across multiple provinces. Iran's Ministry of Health reported 17 people killed and 115 injured in those strikes. Senior U.S. officials, speaking anonymously, said the attacks were carried out by a rogue faction of Iranian hard-liners attempting to sabotage the ceasefire, though Iran's government has insisted its leadership is unified under the new supreme leader and accused Washington of violating the memorandum of understanding signed in June, specifically a clause prohibiting new U.S. sanctions or additional troop deployments to the region.

Despite the escalating rhetoric and renewed military exchanges, diplomatic channels remain open. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi arrived in Muscat, Oman, on Saturday to discuss the Strait of Hormuz and broader peace terms with Omani counterparts, while Qatari mediators traveled to Tehran. The U.S. negotiating team — led by Vice President JD Vance, Jared Kushner, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, and special envoy Steve Witkoff — was expected to participate in parallel talks in Oman. Iran's parliament speaker and lead negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf stated categorically that Tehran would not surrender but called ending the war a global priority, signaling that both sides remain engaged even as ultimatums mount.
